classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
Of the Catholic Church, the Remission of Sins, and the Resurrection of the Flesh.

ccel.title: NPNF1-03. On the Holy Trinity; Doctrinal Treatises; Moral Treatises

source.title: NPNF1-03. On the Holy Trinity; Doctrinal Treatises; Moral Treatises

title: Of the Catholic Church, the Remission of Sins, and the Resurrection of the Flesh.

source.title: NPNF1-03. On the Holy Trinity; Doctrinal Treatises; Moral Treatises

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: —Inasmuch, I repeat, as this is the case, we believe also in The Holy Church, [intending thereby] assuredly the Catholic.

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: For both heretics and schismatics style their congregations churches.

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: But heretics, in holding false opinions regarding God, do injury to the faith itself; while schismatics, on the other hand, in wicked separations break off from brotherly charity, although they may believe just what we believe.

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: Wherefore neither do the heretics belong to the Church catholic, which loves God; nor do the schismatics form a part of the same, inasmuch as it loves the neighbor, and consequently readily forgives the neighbor’s sins, because it prays that forgiveness may be extended to itself by Him who has reconciled us to Himself, doing away with all past things, and calling us to a new life.

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: And until we reach the perfection of this new life, we cannot be without sins.

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: Nevertheless it is a matter of consequence of what sort those sins may be.

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: Neither ought we only to treat of the difference between sins, but we ought most thoroughly to believe that those things in which we sin are in no way forgiven us, if we show ourselves severely unyielding in the matter of forgiving the sins of others.

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: Thus, then, we believe also in The Remission of Sins.

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: And inasmuch as there are three things of which man consists,—namely, spirit, soul, and body,—which again are spoken of as two, because frequently the soul is named along with the spirit; for a certain rational portion of the same, of which beasts are devoid, is called spirit: the principal part in us is the spirit; next, the life whereby we are united with the body is called the soul; finally, the body itself, as it is visible, is the last part in us.

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: This “whole creation” (creatura), however, “groaneth and travaileth until now.”

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: Nevertheless, He has given it the first-fruits of the Spirit, in that it has believed God, and is now of a good will.

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: This spirit is also called the mind, regarding which an apostle speaks thus: “With the mind I serve the law of God.”

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: Which apostle likewise expresses himself thus in another passage: “For God is my witness, whom I serve in my spirit.”

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: Moreover, the soul, when as yet it lusts after carnal good things, is called the flesh.

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: For a certain part thereof resists the Spirit, not in virtue of nature, but in virtue of the custom of sins; whence it is said, “With the mind I serve the law of God, but with the flesh the law of sin.”

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: And this custom has been turned into a nature, according to mortal generation, by the sin of the first man.

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: Consequently it is also written in this wise, “And we were sometime by nature the children of wrath,” that is, of vengeance, through which it has come to pass that we serve the law of sin.

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: The nature of the soul, however, is perfect when it is made subject to its own spirit, and when it follows that spirit as the same follows God.

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: Therefore “the animal man receiveth not the things which are of the Spirit of God.”

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: But the soul is not so speedily subdued to the spirit unto good action, as is the spirit to God unto true faith and goodwill; but sometimes its impetus, whereby it moves downwards into things carnal and temporal, is more tardily bridled.

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: But inasmuch as this same soul is also made pure, and receives the stability of its own nature, under the dominance of the spirit, which is the head for it, which head of the said soul has again its own head in Christ, we ought not to despair of the restoration of the body also to its own proper nature.

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: But this certainly will not be effected so speedily as is the case with the soul; just as the soul too, is not restored so speedily as the spirit.

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: Yet it will take place in the appropriate season, at the last trump, when “the dead shall rise uncorrupted, and we shall be changed.”

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: And accordingly we believe also in The Resurrection of the Flesh, to wit, not merely that that soul, which at present by reason of carnal affections is called the flesh, is restored; but that it shall be so likewise with this visible flesh, which is the flesh according to nature, the name of which has been received by the soul, not in virtue of nature, but in reference to carnal affections: this visible flesh, then, I say, which is the flesh properly so called, must without doubt be believed to be destined to rise again.

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: For the Apostle Paul appears to point to this, as it were, with his finger, when he says, “This corruptible must put on incorruption.”

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: For when he says this, he, as it were, directs his finger toward it.

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: Now it is that which is visible that admits of being pointed out with the finger; since the soul might also have been called corruptible, for it is itself corrupted by vices of manners.

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: And when it is read, “and this mortal [must] put on immortality,” the same visible flesh is signified, inasmuch as at it ever and anon the finger is thus as it were pointed.

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: For the soul also may thus in like manner be called mortal, even as it is designated corruptible in reference to vices of manners.

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: For assuredly it is “the death of the soul to apostatize from God;” which is its first sin in Paradise, as it is contained in the sacred writings.

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: Rise again, therefore, the body will, according to the Christian faith, which is incapable of deceiving.

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: And if this appears incredible to any one, [it is because] he looks simply to what the flesh is at present, while he fails to consider of what nature it shall be hereafter.

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: For at that time of angelic change it will no more be flesh and blood, but only body.

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: For when the apostle speaks of the flesh, he says, “There is one flesh of cattle, another of birds, another of fishes, another of creeping things: there are also both celestial bodies and terrestrial bodies.”

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: Now what he has said here is not “celestial flesh,” but “both celestial bodies and terrestrial bodies.”

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: For all flesh is also body; but every body is not also flesh.

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: In the first instance, [for example, this holds good] in the case of those terrestrial bodies, inasmuch as wood is body, but not flesh.

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: In the case of man, again, or in that of cattle, we have both body and flesh.

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: In the case of celestial bodies, on the other hand, there is no flesh, but only those simple and lucent bodies which the apostle designates spiritual, while some call them ethereal.

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: And consequently, when he says, “Flesh and blood shall not inherit the kingdom of God,” that does not contradict the resurrection of the flesh; but the sentence predicates what will be the nature of that hereafter which at present is flesh and blood.

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: And if any one refuses to believe that the flesh is capable of being changed into the sort of nature thus indicated, he must be led on, step by step, to this faith.

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: For if you require of him whether earth is capable of being changed into water, the nearness of the thing will make it not seem incredible to him.

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: Again, if you inquire whether water is capable of being changed into air, he replies that this also is not absurd, for the elements are near each other.

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: And if, on the subject of the air, it is asked whether that can be changed into an ethereal, that is, a celestial body, the simple fact of the nearness at once convinces him of the possibility of the thing.

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: But if, then, he concedes that through such gradations it is quite a possible thing that earth should be changed into an ethereal body, why does he refuse to believe, when that will of God, too, enters in addition, whereby a human body had power to walk upon the waters, that the same change is capable of being effected with the utmost rapidity, precisely in accordance with the saying, “in the twinkling of an eye,” and without any such gradations, even as, according to common wont, smoke is changed into flame with marvellous quickness?

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: For our flesh assuredly is of earth.

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: But philosophers, on the ground of whose arguments opposition is for the most part offered to the resurrection of the flesh, so far as in these they assert that no terrene body can possibly exist in heaven, yet concede that any kind of body may be converted and changed into every [other] sort of body.

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: And when this resurrection of the body has taken place, being set free then from the condition of time, we shall fully enjoy Eternal Life in ineffable love and steadfastness, without corruption.

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: For “then shall be brought to pass the saying which is written, Death is swallowed up in victory.

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: Where is, O death, thy sting?

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: Where is, O death, thy contention?”

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: This is the faith which in few words is given in the Creed to Christian novices, to be held by them.

verses.book: A Treatise on Faith and the Creed.
verses.text: And these few words are known to the faithful, to the end that in believing they may be made subject to God; that being made subject, they may rightly live; that in rightly living, they may make the heart pure; that with the heart made pure, they may understand that which they believe.
